planting.note: Plant dry corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, rich, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; white trout lily forms colonies slowly by offsets — plant in drifts of 10+ for impact, as single plants are easily lost in the woodland garden.

directions: White trout lily is a native spring ephemeral wildflower of moist deciduous woodlands across the central and eastern United States — its pair of mottled, silvery-green, trout-patterned leaves emerge in early spring and last only until the canopy closes overhead, disappearing completely by June as the plant retreats underground for the rest of the year. The nodding, six-petaled white flowers (with blue or violet anthers) open in April above the characteristic mottled foliage, pollinated primarily by native mining bees and bumblebee queens seeking early-season pollen; the flowers are followed by capsule fruits dispersed by ants (myrmecochory) along with an elaiosome food body the ants consume. White trout lily spreads slowly by underground stolons to form large, showy colonies in undisturbed woodland soil — in ideal conditions, a single planting will gradually expand into drifts of hundreds of plants over decades. Purchase bulbs from native plant nurseries; wild collection is destructive as colonies take 4–7 years to produce the first flower from seed and may not recover from disturbance.

planting.note: Plant only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens — never transplant from the wild (it is illegal in most states and plants almost never survive wild transplanting); plant in fall in acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist, humus-rich, sandy or rocky soil with full mycorrhizal fungal support in partial to full shade.

directions: Pink lady's slipper is perhaps the most iconic wildflower of eastern North American forests — its single inflated, pouched, rose-pink to magenta flower with a central cleft is instantly recognizable and has captured the imagination of generations of woodland hikers. It grows in acidic, humus-rich, sandy or rocky soil under pines, oaks, and hemlocks throughout eastern North America, requiring a very specific soil-dwelling fungus (Rhizoctonia species) to germinate from seed and to survive through its multi-year seedling stage. This fungal dependence, combined with slow growth (plants take 10–15 years to reach blooming size from seed) and sensitivity to transplanting, makes it one of the most challenging native wildflowers to establish; wild plants are strictly protected in most states. Purchase only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens from specialist native orchid nurseries and establish them in an area that has never been disturbed — once established in a suitable location, individual plants may persist and bloom for 50 or more years. Never collect from the wild.

planting.note: Plant only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens from specialist orchid nurseries in fall; yellow lady's slipper is the most adaptable of the native Cypripedium species and can succeed in more garden conditions than pink lady's slipper — plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade.

directions: Yellow lady's slipper is the most widely distributed and arguably the most garden-adaptable of all native North American orchids — growing in moist, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soils across an enormous range from Canada to the Gulf Coast states, and tolerating a wider range of pH and soil conditions than the highly acid-demanding pink lady's slipper. The golden-yellow, pouch-shaped flowers with maroon-striped lateral petals and a rich, sweet-spicy fragrance emerge in May from stems bearing 1–3 blooms; bumblebee queens are the primary pollinators, entering the pouch to discover there is no nectar and escaping through a back exit that brushes them with pollen — a deceptive but effective pollination mechanism. Like all Cypripedium, it forms a long-lived clump that slowly expands by rhizomes; established plants purchased from nurseries and planted in appropriate woodland conditions can persist for decades, eventually forming spectacular multi-stemmed flowering colonies. The most rewarding and accessible native terrestrial orchid for the shaded woodland garden when purchased as tissue-cultured nursery stock.

planting.note: Plant only nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured specimens in fall in moist to wet, neutral to slightly alkaline, calcium-rich, humus-rich soil in partial shade; showy lady's slipper prefers cooler, wetter conditions and neutral to basic pH (pH 5.5–7.5) unlike most native orchids that need acidic soil.

directions: Showy lady's slipper is the largest and most spectacularly beautiful of all North American native orchids — the official state wildflower of Minnesota — with large, pure white upper petals surrounding a dramatically inflated, rosy-pink to magenta pouch that is rivaled in size only by tropical orchids. Unlike most eastern orchids that need acidic soil, showy lady's slipper prefers neutral to slightly alkaline, calcium-rich soils in cool, moist, shaded swamp forests, tamarack bogs, and calcareous seeps — a habitat preference that makes it more restrictive than yellow lady's slipper but potentially suitable for gardens with moist, organic, neutral soil in zones 2–7. Individual plants are remarkably long-lived and slow-growing, capable of persisting in the same location for 50–100 years when undisturbed, slowly expanding into multi-stemmed clumps of increasing grandeur. As with all native terrestrial orchids, purchase only tissue-cultured nursery propagated specimens — wild collection of any Cypripedium is illegal in most states and ecologically devastating. Note: handling the foliage can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals.

planting.note: Plant corms from nursery-propagated sources in spring in wet, acidic, peaty or sandy, nutrient-poor soil in full sun to partial shade; grass pink orchid requires an open, wet, acidic bog or seep environment with full sun to partial shade and nutrient-poor conditions.

directions: Grass pink orchid is one of the most common and widely distributed of all eastern North American bog orchids — a single grasslike leaf and an upright flower stem bearing up to 10 vivid magenta-pink flowers with a distinctive bearded lip appear in late spring and early summer in acidic, wet bogs, seepage slopes, pitcher plant savannas, and longleaf pine flatwoods across the Gulf Coastal Plain. Unlike most orchids, grass pink orchid grows from small, underground corms that can be purchased from specialist native plant nurseries and planted in appropriate bog garden conditions, making it more accessible than most native terrestrial orchids. The flowers practice deceptive pollination — the bearded yellow lip (which resembles a pollen-covered stamen to naive bees) flips downward when a bee lands, dropping the bee onto the actual pollen masses on the column below; bees learn quickly and return infrequently, so the plant depends on a steady supply of inexperienced native bees. An important component of Gulf Coast bog gardens when combined with pitcher plants (Sarracenia), sundews, and bog sedges in full-sun, wet, acidic soil.

planting.note: Plant only nursery-propagated specimens in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, nutrient-poor soil in full sun to partial shade; yellow fringed orchid requires the acidic, wet soils of bogs, seepage slopes, and moist pine flatwoods — plant in partnership with appropriate mycorrhizal fungi.

directions: Yellow fringed orchid is among the most spectacularly beautiful of all North American native wildflowers — a magnificent spike of dozens of bright golden-orange to deep orange flowers, each with a dramatically fringed lower lip that creates a feathery, lace-like appearance unique among wildflowers, blooming in July and August in open bogs, moist savannas, and wet pine flatwoods throughout the Gulf Coastal Plain and eastern United States. The long, slender nectar spur extending from each flower can only be reached by long-tongued swallowtail butterflies (especially tiger and spicebush swallowtails), which are the primary pollinators — the match between floral anatomy and pollinator tongue length is so precise that no other insect can effectively pollinate the flowers. Found throughout the Gulf Coast longleaf pine flatwoods and seepage bogs from Texas to Florida, where it blooms in late summer heat alongside pitcher plants, sundews, and other carnivorous plants of this unique, fire-maintained ecosystem. An unforgettable sight when in full bloom and worth every effort to establish in a properly maintained bog garden.

planting.note: Plant only nursery-propagated corms in fall in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich, undisturbed soil in full to partial shade under mature deciduous hardwoods; crane-fly orchid requires an undisturbed, leaf-littered woodland floor with appropriate mycorrhizal fungi.

directions: Crane-fly orchid is one of the most ecologically fascinating native orchids of eastern North American forests — a shade-dwelling terrestrial orchid that shows a remarkable seasonal reversal: a single, attractively mottled, purple-green leaf emerges in fall, remains through winter as a solitary, easy-to-overlook woodland groundcover, then withers and disappears in late spring as the leafless flowering stalk emerges in midsummer bearing 20–40 tiny, translucent, crane-fly-shaped flowers that give the species its name. This reversed phenology — leafy in winter, leafless when flowering in summer — is unique among North American orchids and perfectly adapted to deciduous forest light patterns: the leaf photosynthesizes during winter when canopy trees are leafless, storing energy in the underground corm for summer flowering. Nocturnal moths are the primary pollinators, attracted by a faint fragrance at night. A subtle, quiet orchid of the undisturbed forest floor that rewards patient observers; best appreciated in its natural woodland setting rather than cultivated, though nursery-propagated corms are occasionally available from specialist orchid growers.

planting.note: Plant only nursery-propagated specimens in fall in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in full to partial shade under conifer or hardwood canopy; rattlesnake plantain requires an undisturbed, leaf-littered woodland floor with appropriate mycorrhizal support.

directions: Downy rattlesnake plantain is among the most attractive native orchids for year-round woodland interest — its distinctive rosettes of dark green leaves with a striking network of silver-white veins create a bold, graphic pattern reminiscent of a rattlesnake's scale markings that makes each plant an eye-catching feature of the forest floor throughout the year, regardless of the brief summer bloom. An evergreen, low-growing orchid of the leaf litter under eastern hemlock, pine, and mixed hardwood forests across a broad range from Canada to the Gulf Coastal Plain, it is one of the most shade-tolerant of all native orchids and thrives in the darkest parts of mature forest understory. In summer, a fuzzy, upright flower spike produces small, white, globular flowers pollinated by bumblebees; the spike can reach 16 inches tall and is ornamental in its own right. The striking foliage pattern was historically associated with snakebite medicine by some indigenous peoples — hence the 'rattlesnake plantain' name — though there is no medicinal efficacy. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants; wild plants depend on mycorrhizal fungi and rarely survive transplanting.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated rhizome divisions in spring in saturated to wet, acidic (pH 3.5–5.5), nutrient-poor, peaty or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; rose pogonia is a true bog obligate that will not survive in typical garden soil — it requires constantly wet, open, acidic bog conditions with no fertilizer.

directions: Rose pogonia is a delicate and charming bog orchid native to open, acidic wetlands — bogs, bog meadows, seeps, and sphagnum-filled depressions — across eastern North America from Newfoundland to Florida and west to Texas, where it blooms in June and July with a single rose-pink flower bearing a conspicuous fringed and bearded lip that smells faintly of raspberries. Like grass pink orchid, it practices deceptive pollination, offering no reward to pollinators — native bumblebees visit the flower expecting nectar but find none, and only return occasionally due to the lack of reward, which is sufficient for cross-pollination between widely spaced plants in bog meadows. Rose pogonia is one of the more tractable native bog orchids for cultivation because it spreads vegetatively by horizontal rhizomes in appropriate bog conditions, gradually forming small colonies in sphagnum moss or wet, peaty soil without the extreme mycorrhizal fungal dependence of many other native orchids. An essential component of a native bog garden in zones 3–9 alongside pitcher plants, sundews, and butterworts in nutrient-poor, constantly wet, acidic conditions.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated tubers or transplants in fall in moist to moderately wet, acidic to neutral, well-drained to moist soil in full sun to partial shade; nodding ladies' tresses is the most adaptable of all native terrestrial orchids and can succeed in a wider range of conditions than most, including moist meadows and roadsides.

directions: Nodding ladies' tresses is the most common and widely distributed of all North American native orchids — a fall-blooming terrestrial orchid found in moist meadows, roadsides, and open woodland edges across virtually the entire eastern United States, producing delicate spiral-whorled spikes of small, pure white, vanilla-scented flowers that are a surprise and delight when discovered blooming in September and October when orchids are the last thing most people expect to find flowering. Its late-season bloom coincides with the final nectar demand of native bumblebee queens preparing for winter, and the white, fragrant flowers are heavily visited by yellow-and-black native bumblebees that are attracted from considerable distance by the fragrance. Unlike most native terrestrial orchids, nodding ladies' tresses is found in a wide variety of habitats including disturbed sites, roadsides, golf courses, and moist lawns, making it potentially the most accessible native orchid for cultivation — it has established itself from naturally windblown seed in appropriate garden conditions without deliberate planting. A beautiful orchid available from specialist native nurseries that can succeed in average moist garden conditions when other native orchids would fail.
